title: Finding household water leaks
subtitle: Using the meter method and visual checks to locate waste
excerpt: A dripping tap wastes around 5500 litres per year. A running toilet cistern can waste 400 litres per day. Neither is visible. The water meter method identifies whether a leak exists; a systematic check of each fitting locates it.
sourceNotes: Waterwise UK household leakage guidance. Water UK consumer water efficiency guidance. Building Research Establishment (BRE) guidance on internal plumbing leak detection.

content.text: UK household water use averages approximately 150 litres per person per day. A 3-person household uses approximately 165,000 litres per year. A running toilet cistern adds up to 146,000 litres per year to that: nearly doubling consumption without any visible change. Finding and fixing leaks is the highest-return water saving action, and costs nothing beyond the repair materials.
If you have a water meter, this method confirms whether a leak exists anywhere on the supply side of the stop valve.
Turn off all water-using appliances (washing machine, dishwasher) and ensure no one uses the taps or flushes any toilet.
Read the meter and note the exact reading, including the low-flow (red) dial if present.
Wait 30 minutes without using any water.
Read the meter again. If the reading has changed, water is being consumed continuously. There is a leak or a constantly running appliance.

A running cistern is the most common significant household leak. The water runs silently down the back of the pan and into the drain. To check: add a few drops of food colouring to the cistern tank. Wait 10 minutes without flushing. If the coloured water appears in the bowl, the cistern is leaking through the flapper valve. Most flappers are a push-fit replacement costing a few pounds from a hardware shop.

A dripping tap that fills a 200 ml glass in 15 minutes is losing approximately 5500 litres per year. Turn all taps fully off and observe for 5 minutes. A ceramic disc cartridge that drips is typically replaced as a complete cartridge (varies by tap brand; available from plumbing merchants). A traditional pillar tap uses a rubber washer that degrades with use and is replaced by shutting off the supply, removing the tap head, and replacing the washer.

Boiler pressure relief valve (PRV): a small pipe from the boiler to an external drain. If dripping constantly, the boiler system pressure is too high or the PRV is failing. A condensing boiler should hold 1.0 to 1.5 bar; above 2.5 bar the PRV activates. Re-pressurise or investigate with a plumber if the system cannot hold steady pressure.
Outside tap: frequently left partially open or with a failed washer. Check in winter particularly when not in regular use.

Supply pipe between meter and house: if the meter shows leakage but no internal fittings are found, the leak is in the supply pipe. This is the responsibility of the householder from the boundary stop valve. Water companies offer a free leak repair service for the section from the main to the boundary in most areas. Knowing where your
is going is the first step.

items.fix: Check outdoor taps and irrigation timer settings first. If none, check the stop valve at the boundary; turn it off and re-read the meter. If the meter stops, the leak is between the boundary stop valve and the house.
items.cause: Leak is in the underground supply pipe or the supply feeds an external irrigation system left on
items.symptom: Meter moves but no internal leak found

items.fix: Check the water level in the cistern: it should sit 25 mm below the top of the overflow pipe. Bend the float arm down slightly if the level is too high.
items.cause: The overflow or siphon is also faulty, or the water level is set too high (water flows over the overflow pipe)
items.symptom: Cistern runs but flapper has been replaced
